script:
Hello, I am Josh Weinberg and this is The Portfolio Podcast. Tuesday, as of July 7 2026 at 4:56 AM ET — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio sits at one hundred one thousand two hundred seventy-seven dollars and eleven cents, up one point two eight percent on the session, a gain of one thousand two hundred seventy-seven dollars and eleven cents from the one hundred thousand dollar base.

Apple, A-A-P-L, just posted a six point two one percent single-session gain, closing above three hundred twelve dollars. That is not noise. That is the largest move among all nine holdings in Tuesday's session, driven by a confirmed chip partnership extension with Broadcom through 2031 and a foldable iPhone roadmap that has analysts asking one pointed question: is the rally already priced in, or is this just the beginning?

Broad market context for Tuesday's session was shaped by divergence within the technology sector. Semiconductor names faced pressure — South Korea's Kospi dropped six percent, led by chip stocks including Samsung Electronics and S-K Hynix, as investors questioned whether artificial intelligence demand can sustain record earnings growth. Meanwhile, large-cap software and consumer technology names held firmer ground, with the Nasdaq-100 outperforming on a relative basis, lifted primarily by Apple's outsized move. Federal Reserve credibility also entered the conversation, with President Trump once again publicly targeting the Federal Open Market Committee — a macro overhang that rattled rate-sensitive positioning and kept bond markets on alert.

The headline story of Tuesday's session belongs to Apple, A-A-P-L, and it is more layered than a single data point. Apple extended its custom chip supply partnership with Broadcom, A-V-G-O, through 2031, locking in multi-year ASIC commitments that analysts say meaningfully improve revenue visibility for both companies. The deal was reported by finance.yahoo.com at one twenty-seven AM Eastern. Separately, Apple's broader iPhone roadmap came into focus, with reporting confirming development of a first foldable model and a new Ultra tier device — product lines that represent entirely new revenue categories for the company. Apple's share price has now climbed ten point nine seven percent over the past week and twenty-three point three four percent over the past ninety days, per data cited at twelve thirty-nine AM Eastern. The one-year total shareholder return stands at forty-nine point five one percent. At eight point nine seven percent of the QQQM portfolio, this is the single largest holding, which means Tuesday's six point two one percent gain was the dominant driver of the portfolio's overall performance. The question analysts are now posing publicly is whether those gains are already fully reflected in the price. That is the tension heading into the rest of the week.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, starting at the top of the weight stack.

Microsoft, M-S-F-T, closed at three hundred eighty-six dollars and seventy-four cents, up zero point six four percent on the session, a gain of two dollars and forty-six cents. At eight point four two percent of the portfolio, this was a steady if unspectacular session for the name. A Seeking Alpha piece published at four twenty AM Eastern called Microsoft a, quote, A-I diamond that is stronger than semiconductors, arguing that value in artificial intelligence is shifting from scarce hardware to Microsoft's recurring software and cloud revenue model — framing it as a strong buy. On the other side of the ledger, the Wall Street Journal reported that Microsoft's Game Pass has approximately thirty million subscribers, well below an internal projection of seventy-seven million. Microsoft responded by cutting more than three thousand Xbox jobs. That is a meaningful operational signal — the consumer gaming segment is underperforming internal targets by a wide margin. The A-I software thesis remains intact, but the gaming overhang is a real data point to monitor. A leadership change at N-V-D-A, with former Microsoft sales executive Nicholas Parker set to take over worldwide field operations, also tied Microsoft's name into the broader semiconductor rotation narrative on Tuesday.

Over at Nvidia, N-V-D-A, the session told a different story. The stock closed at one hundred ninety-five dollars and fifty-five cents, down one point zero three percent, a loss of two dollars and three cents. At eight point one five percent of the portfolio, that decline is not catastrophic, but the trend line deserves attention. The seven-day return is down two point two seven percent, and the thirty-day return is down four point six six percent, even as the ninety-day figure remains positive at seven point four zero percent. Nvidia named Nicholas Parker, formerly of Microsoft, as its new worldwide field operations chief, replacing the retiring Ajay K. Puri. The leadership transition came as macro pressure built around the Federal Reserve — President Trump's latest public criticism of the F-O-M-C, reported at four twenty-six AM Eastern, introduces uncertainty around rate policy at a moment when growth-stock valuations are sensitive to any shift in the rate outlook. The Korean chip selloff added further pressure. Watch the ninety-day support level closely.

Alphabet, G-O-O-G-L, closed at three hundred sixty-six dollars and forty-six cents, up one point four five percent, a gain of five dollars and twenty-five cents. At eight point zero one percent of the portfolio, this was a solid session. Two notable developments: Nigeria's president directed the country's Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Commission to investigate Alphabet and other tech firms over alleged anti-competitive practices and unauthorized use of news content, per a report at four thirty-eight AM Eastern. That regulatory headline is worth flagging — it is small in isolation but part of a broader pattern of global regulatory pressure on large-cap tech. On the positive side, Google participated in a four hundred eleven million euro funding round for Proxima Fusion, a clean energy startup planning a commercial fusion plant. That is a long-duration bet, but it speaks to Alphabet's appetite for frontier technology investment.

Amazon, A-M-Z-N, closed at two hundred forty-four dollars and sixteen cents, up one point zero two percent, a gain of two dollars and forty-six cents. At five point five two percent of the portfolio, Tuesday's move was constructive. No specific company-level news drove the session — the gain tracked the broader large-cap technology bid. The cloud and advertising revenue mix continues to be the fundamental anchor for this name heading into the next earnings cycle.

Broadcom, A-V-G-O, closed at three hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety cents, up one point two three percent, a gain of four dollars and fifty-six cents. At four point six one percent of the portfolio, the Apple chip deal extension through 2031 was the clear catalyst. Finance.yahoo.com reported at one twenty-seven AM Eastern that the extended partnership improves revenue visibility and deepens Broadcom's A-I custom silicon exposure. The ninety-day return stands at eleven point nine six percent, and the one-year total shareholder return is thirty-seven point three seven percent. The Apple relationship is now locked in at multi-year custom ASIC commitments — a structural positive for revenue predictability.

Meanwhile, Meta Platforms, M-E-T-A, was the weakest large-cap name in Tuesday's session. The stock closed at six hundred dollars and twenty-nine cents, down two point zero six percent, a loss of twelve dollars and sixty-two cents. At four point four eight percent of the portfolio, that decline carries real weight. Nigeria's regulatory investigation directly named Meta alongside Alphabet, adding a fresh layer of international legal exposure. No specific earnings or product catalyst offset the pressure. The six hundred dollar level is now a line in the sand technically — a break below and sustained close there would be meaningful to watch.

Tesla, T-S-L-A, closed at four hundred nineteen dollars and seventy-seven cents, down one point three zero percent, a loss of five dollars and fifty-three cents. At three point five four percent of the portfolio, the session loss was modest in dollar terms. No company-specific news drove Tuesday's move. The decline tracked the broader risk-off tone in consumer discretionary and growth names, consistent with macro pressure from the Fed narrative and the Korean chip-led selloff. The next catalyst for Tesla remains its next production and delivery data release.

Costco, C-O-S-T, closed at nine hundred fifty dollars and twenty-five cents, up two point seven seven percent, a gain of twenty-five dollars and fifty-eight cents. At two point eight one percent of the portfolio and the only consumer staples name in the group, Costco was a standout on Tuesday. No specific news drove the move, but a two point seven seven percent gain in a session where growth names faced pressure is a clear signal of rotation into defensive consumer names. The nine hundred fifty dollar level is a new milestone for the stock.

Three numbers to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any Federal Reserve speaker comments scheduled for Wednesday — given President Trump's renewed public pressure on the F-O-M-C reported Tuesday morning, any Fed official response on rate policy independence will move rate-sensitive growth names across this portfolio. Second, SpaceX's full trading and index weight integration into the Nasdaq-100 this week — Invesco Q-Q-Q holders now carry indirect SpaceX exposure, and early volatility in that name will ripple into Q-Q-Q-M price action. Third, any follow-on analyst notes on Apple's foldable iPhone timeline and the Broadcom ASIC deal — the pricing question raised Tuesday morning will drive institutional positioning decisions over the next two sessions.

That is Tuesday's session. This is Josh Weinberg for The Portfolio Podcast. The next episode drops at five PM Eastern on the next trading day. Keep your eye on the Federal Reserve response, Apple's post-rally price discovery, and whether Meta can defend that six hundred dollar level. Stay disciplined.
